element_introduction_order.mastery_context: player chooses initial faction (Spanish Empire, Dutch Republic, Kingdom of England, or French)
player selects starting year (1560-1680) which determines political/economic landscape and ship availability
player learns to navigate Caribbean map with invisible barriers; Spanish Main, Central America, Gulf of Mexico, islands
player engages with ship-to-ship combat or avoidance; learns weather gage, vessel types (frigate, pinnace), tactical positioning

element_introduction_order.first_appears: during town raids or boarding actions
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: player practices melee combat mechanics during land incursions and crew combat
player learns city statistics (wealth, population), buying/selling goods, profit optimization

element_introduction_order.first_appears: ongoing from start, becomes apparent through gameplay
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: player navigates shifting alliances, war/peace cycles between four nations, gains rank and land through reputation
player seeks rare pirate encounters for universal reputation gains
player locates and rescues long-lost relatives
player makes strategic marital choices affecting rank and accomplishments
player manages retirement window; aging reduces combat effectiveness and crew recruitment
game evaluates player wealth, rank, marital status, accomplishments; assigns final position (beggar to King's advisor)

typical_relief_triggers: successful ship capture or trade completion
port arrival and city trading interface
era-based economic shifts that create new opportunities
peaceful periods allowing pure trading focus
typical_tension_triggers: unexpected random events affecting economic/political conditions
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Open-ended emergent pacing; player-driven with ambient environmental pressure from aging mechanic and time passage; tension builds gradually as character ages and options narrow; relief comes through successful accumulation of wealth, rank, and objectives

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: player freedom to pursue multiple narrative threads (piracy, privateering, treasure-seeking, family rescue, pure trade); no forced progression path

difficulty_curve.early_game: variable based on chosen era; 1560 start presents Spanish-dominated, quieter Caribbean with fewer opportunities; 1680 start presents mature trading networks and more aggressive competition
difficulty_curve.mid_game: difficulty increases through random diplomatic shifts, economic volatility, and escalating crew recruitment costs; player must adapt strategy to changing political landscape
difficulty_curve.late_game: sharp difficulty spike from aging mechanic; character loses combat effectiveness and health deteriorates; crew recruitment becomes nearly impossible; game forces retirement
notable_spikes: era selection choice dramatically affects overall difficulty trajectory
transition from early peace to war periods (if applicable)
late-game aging curve that limits tactical options
random nation relation collapses that eliminate safe trading partners

notable_level_patterns.description: Cities operate as living economic entities with fluctuating wealth and population; player must monitor statistics and time trading decisions strategically around market conditions
Three distinct combat modes tested across different skills: hand-eye coordination (fencing), tactical ability (sea combat with weather/positioning), strategic thinking (crew/resource management)
Player actions against ships/towns create persistent diplomatic consequences; allegiances shift based on accumulated reputation; enables emergent narrative
Six different starting years (1560, 1600, 1620, 1640, 1660, 1680) fundamentally reshape available opportunities, ship types, and nation power balance; encourages replay
